---
title: "0 · Structural model"
---

The **structural model** is the fine, time-invariant description of *what the subsurface is* — its
terrain, soils, geology, and mechanical stiffness. These are the **static layers**: the prior that sets
what the ground *can* hold, where water sits, and how stiff it can be. The dynamic states (water table,
soil moisture, velocity change) are built by modulating this fixed skeleton in time; this chapter is
about how the skeleton itself is aggregated and delivered.

Static layers are the only inputs that carry genuine **90 m spatial texture** — the coarse dynamic
drivers (precipitation, satellites) cannot. So the whole product's spatial resolution is inherited from
these layers, which is why building them correctly, and at the right resolution, comes first.

## Building the static layers

Each layer is fetched from its authoritative source, cleaned, reprojected to the analysis CRS
(EPSG:5070), and resampled to the common 90 m grid. The build steps:

- **Terrain** — the USGS **3DEP** DEM (10 m) is the parent. From it we derive **HAND** (Height Above
  Nearest Drainage — 0 in valley floors, large on ridges), **TWI** (topographic wetness index,
  $\ln(a/\tan\beta)$), and **slope**, using flow routing on the DEM before downsampling to 90 m.
- **Soil** — **SOLUS100** (100 m, USDA-NRCS ML digital soil mapping) supplies the hydromechanical
  texture set: clay / sand / silt fractions, pH, CEC, oven-dry bulk density, and depth-to-lithic (soil
  thickness). Windowed cloud-optimised reads, averaged over the near-surface horizon.
- **Near-surface stiffness** — **Vs30**, preferred from the measurement-based **Soil Velocity Model**
  (Grant, Wirth & Stone 2025) where staged, otherwise the Wald–Allen topographic-slope proxy on the
  90 m 3DEP slope (USGS grid / Sanger–Maurer parametric are further fallbacks).
- **Geology / lithology** — surficial-geology polygons rasterised to 90 m through a lithology crosswalk,
  giving a categorical material class per cell.
- **Subsurface geometry** — **depth-to-bedrock** and **distance-to-coast**, resampled to 90 m; combined
  with HAND and lithology into a **hydrogeologic-domain** mask that stratifies the later models.

Every step is provenance-tracked (source → operation → native resolution → target resolution), so a
downstream consumer can see exactly how each 90 m layer was made.

## Target resolution: 90 m

The target is **90 m, EPSG:5070** — set by the hazard, not the data. Liquefaction in valley fill,
shallow landslides on hillslopes, and compound flooding all resolve at ~100 m or finer, so a coarser
grid would blur the valley-vs-ridge distinction that governs the hazard. Two consequences follow:

- **Fine sources are downsampled, coarse sources are upsampled — honestly.** 3DEP (10 m) is aggregated
  to 90 m (a genuine down-sample); SOLUS (100 m) is resampled to 90 m (near-identity); a coarse layer
  reprojected up to 90 m gains *no* new information, and the provenance records that so a resampled
  cell is never mistaken for a native observation.
- **90 m is the resolution the dynamic states inherit.** Because the coarse precipitation/satellite
  drivers carry the *time* signal but not the *space* texture, the 90 m static envelope is what gives
  the final water-table, soil-moisture, and stiffness fields their spatial detail.

## The static-layer catalog

The static layers needed for the soil-hydromechanical state and the downstream hazards, each built to
the same 90 m analysis grid (@fig-static-layers shows the **real** data over the Puget Sound / Cascades
pilot):

![**The static-layer catalog — real 90 m data.** Nine of the static layers over the Puget Sound /
Cascades analysis footprint, all on the 90 m EPSG:5070 grid: terrain derivatives HAND / slope / TWI
(from the 3DEP DEM), the SOLUS100 soil texture (clay / sand), the Saxton–Rawls hydraulic envelope
(saturated conductivity Ksat, field-capacity θ, available water capacity AWC), and the Vs30 (here the
Wald–Allen fallback; the measurement-based Soil Velocity Model is preferred where staged). Water bodies
and cells outside the terrain footprint are masked. Regenerate with
`pixi run static-layers` (reads `data/processed/`).](assets/static_layers.png){#fig-static-layers}

Additional static layers used by the pipeline but not shown above (lithology / geology, depth-to-bedrock,
distance-to-coast, and the derived hydrogeologic-domain mask) are built the same way and enter the
domain stratification.

| Layer | Measures | Source (native → 90 m) | Role in hydromechanics / hazards |
|---|---|---|---|
| HAND | height above nearest drainage | 3DEP DEM 10 m → 90 m | shallow water-table predictor; valley-fill = liquefaction, hillslope = landslide |
| TWI | topographic wetness $\ln(a/\tan\beta)$ | 3DEP 10 m → 90 m | TOPMODEL lateral-flow redistribution of the water table |
| Slope | surface gradient | 3DEP 10 m → 90 m | infinite-slope Factor of Safety; Vs30 proxy input |
| Clay / sand / silt | soil texture fractions | SOLUS100 100 m → 90 m | Saxton–Rawls hydraulic envelope; friction / cohesion |
| pH · CEC · bulk density | soil chemistry / density | SOLUS100 100 m → 90 m | K_sat pedotransfer; density in $V_S=\sqrt{\mu/\rho}$ |
| Soil thickness | depth to lithic contact | SOLUS100 100 m → 90 m | storage capacity; transmissivity $T=K_{sat}h$ |
| Lithology / geology | surficial material class | geology polygons → 90 m raster | hydrogeologic domain; stiffness control |
| Vs30 | 30 m time-averaged $V_S$ | Soil Velocity Model (Grant, Wirth & Stone 2025); Wald–Allen fallback → 90 m | NEHRP site class; liquefaction stiffness |
| Depth to bedrock | subsurface geometry | subsurface product → 90 m | aquifer bottom; domain stratification |
| Distance to coast | coastal proximity | derived → 90 m | sea-level / tidal boundary conditioning |

Together these form the static envelope; the [Input data](01-input-data.qmd) chapter covers the
dynamic ground-sensor and satellite streams that modulate it, and the [Physics](03a-physics-hydrology.qmd)
chapters show how the envelope enters the water budget and the petrophysical rheology.
